data.title: ImmuCell signals capacity expansion to $30M+ and launches new First Defense formats as backlog declines
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ImmuCell Corporation (ICCC) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Michael Brigham, President and CEO, stated that ImmuCell's business is becoming larger, more diverse, and more complex, describing the recent growth in First Defense sales as "very positive for us." He highlighted the completion of investments to increase production capacity above $30 million per year, noting, “expanding our production facilities and implementing the new equipment was a huge project. We implemented important process improvements and worked through certain contamination events. It is important to note that we have not incurred another contamination event for over a year now.” Brigham also emphasized achieving gross margin expansion alongside revenue growth and an increased cash balance of about $4.6 million as of March 31, 2025. He pointed to traction for new First Defense product formats, which now constitute a suite of related offerings. Brigham introduced investigational product use of Re-Tain to collect market feedback and reduce product development expenses while exploring strategic options for the technology. He added, “growing our senior management team brings added expense, but also offers new opportunities and benefits.”
  • Tim Fiori, Chief Financial Officer, stated, “Product sales during the first quarter of 2025 increased 11% or $810,000 over the first quarter of 2024 to a record $8.1 million. Those record quarterly sales eclipsed the previous record set in the fourth quarter of 2024.” Fiori highlighted the reduction of the order backlog from $4.4 million at the end of December 2024 to $4 million at the end of March 2025, and further to $3.4 million as of May 6, 2025. He confirmed that annual production capacity has reached the $30 million target, with $15.8 million in sales over the six-month period ended March 31, 2025. Fiori noted a 28% year-over-year increase in product sales for the twelve months ended March 31, 2025, reaching $27.3 million. He reported a gross margin of 42% in the first quarter of 2025, up from 37% in the fourth quarter of 2024, and called for continued progress toward the 45% gross margin target. Fiori noted “adjusted EBITDA of $2.3 million, $3.7 million and $3.3 million during the three-month, six-month and 12-month periods ended March 31, 2025,” a marked improvement from prior-year periods.

Outlook

  • Management indicated optimism for the remainder of 2025, referencing both increased capacity and continuing backlog reduction. Brigham stated, “we are optimistic as we work our way through the balance of 2025.” He described the ongoing launch of new First Defense product formats and the investigational field use of Re-Tain as key near-term initiatives. Brigham also mentioned the aim to achieve a gross margin of 45% or more, with current progress reflecting “more work to do.”
  • The company did not provide explicit numeric forward guidance for future quarters but signaled expectations for continued recovery, backlog reduction, and further product line expansion into bulk powder formats later in 2025.

Financial Results

  • ImmuCell reported product sales of $8.1 million for Q1 2025, representing an 11% increase over the prior year’s first quarter and setting a new quarterly record. The order backlog declined from $4.4 million as of December 31, 2024, to $4 million at March 31, 2025, and further to $3.4 million by May 6, 2025. Product sales for the twelve months ended March 31, 2025, rose 28% to $27.3 million. Gross margin improved to 42% in Q1 2025 from 37% in Q4 2024. Adjusted EBITDA for the three, six, and twelve months ended March 31, 2025, was $2.3 million, $3.7 million, and $3.3 million, respectively, compared to $458,000, $247,000, and negative $280,000 for the corresponding periods in the previous year. Cash balance increased to about $4.6 million as of March 31, 2025.

Q&A

  • George Melas, MKH Management: Questioned the contribution of the new bulk powder product and the impact of Tri-Shield’s sales mix on gross margin. Michael Brigham explained the bulk powder format is still in development and is expected to reach the market over the next three quarters, with no sales yet reported. He noted that “Tri-Shield has been a big seller since we launched it ... the broader claim set really makes that product excel.” On gross margin, Brigham stated that Tri-Shield is more expensive to produce, but higher volumes and fixed cost leverage drove the margin improvement to 42%, with room for further gains.
  • Russ Tolander, Capital Alliance: Sought clarification on EBITDA erosion from last year’s contamination event and future expectations. Brigham stated, “contamination, you’re remembering it right, April 2024 was the last one ... the revenue rebound began in the fourth quarter of 2024.” Tolander also inquired about Re-Tain updates and the effect of product development spending on core business profitability. Brigham described investigational product use for Re-Tain as a “really important development” that will generate field data but not revenue in the near term, pending full FDA approval.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Asked about the difference between investigational use and product approval, and whether recent profitability would reduce the need for future equity sales. Brigham explained that investigational use allows for non-revenue-generating field studies and stated, “we monitor that closely. I’d like to refer to the ATM as we’re opportunistic ... we just have a lot—it’s a great tool to be, because of its flexibility.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a positive to slightly optimistic tone, congratulating management on improved results, probing for detail on product development, margin trends, and future profitability. Their questions focused on sustainability of recent improvements and the outlook for new products and revenue streams.
  • Management maintained a confident, transparent, and slightly cautious tone. Brigham highlighted operational improvements, strategic product launches, and the mitigation of past disruptions. He acknowledged challenges and the need for continued execution, using phrases such as “we are optimistic,” “work in progress,” and “still got room for improvement.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone has shifted from cautious optimism and recovery to a more confident outlook, with both analysts and management focusing on forward momentum and product pipeline execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter features a newly appointed CFO and increased senior management investment. Gross margin improved from 37% in Q4 2024 to 42% in Q1 2025, with management reiterating the 45% target. Product sales set consecutive records in Q4 2024 and Q1 2025, driven by strong Tri-Shield performance. Backlog is declining, reflecting increased production capacity. The product mix discussion was more nuanced, with Tri-Shield’s sales share rising to 71% in Q1 2025. Strategic focus has shifted from overcoming contamination events to capacity ramp and product portfolio expansion, particularly the bulk powder initiative and investigational use of Re-Tain. Analyst questions have evolved from understanding gross margin drivers and inventory concerns to probing new product commercialization timelines and long-term profitability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management emphasized the need to “avoid significant contamination events and equipment breakdowns and operate with strong production yields.” The potential for future operational disruptions remains a key risk. There was acknowledgment of elevated but controlled administrative and product development expenses. Brigham highlighted the dependency on FDA approval for Re-Tain, noting delays related to contract manufacturer inspections. Cash needs and potential future equity issuance were discussed as ongoing considerations, though recent profitability has reduced urgency. Analysts raised concerns about margin sustainability and the timing and impact of new product launches, particularly for the bulk powder format and Re-Tain.

Final Takeaway

ImmuCell’s management underscored strong sales momentum, improved gross margin, and a return to operational stability following a period of production disruptions. With record quarterly sales, ongoing backlog reduction, and expansion into new First Defense product formats, the company signals confidence in achieving further growth. The launch of investigational field use for Re-Tain and movement toward a 45% gross margin target reflect a focus on both near-term execution and strategic innovation, while risk management and capital discipline remain in clear focus.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SPX Technologies rated Buy in new coverage at B. Riley Securities
data.description:
SPX Technologies (NYSE:SPXC) late Wednesday was rated as a Buy in new research coverage at B. Riley Securities. Analyst Jeff Van Sinderen highlighted the company's track record of organic and acquisition-driven growth, high-margin business model and increasing use of AI-enabled digital solutions.

“SPXC is a global designer, manufacturer, installer and service provider for highly specialized, engineered solutions in HVAC and detection and measurement (D&M), with an impressive track record of profitably growing organically and through acquisitions,” Van Sinderen wrote in a May 14 report to clients. “We expect that to continue.”

The firm emphasized SPX’s strategic focus on remaining at the upper end of the value chain by offering sustainability-enhanced infrastructure products and market-leading brands. According to Van Sinderen, about two-thirds of SPX’s (NYSE:SPXC) revenue comes from replacement sales, and nearly 90% of its revenue is Cdriven by brands that hold either the number one or two position in their markets.

SPX’s (SPXC) profitability is also supported by scale advantages and operational efficiency, including the application of 80/20 principles to streamline performance. Notably, the company is still in the early stages of a digital transformation that is boosting high-margin recurring revenue -- particularly through software-enabled detection and measurement products.

“As D&M offerings are effectively harnessing the advantages of AI, including predictive analytics and software algorithms, we expect the application of AI to continue increasing the value of the company’s solutions,” Van Sinderen said.

Over the past four years, SPX (SPXC) has deployed approximately 90% of its capital into strategic acquisitions. Since 2015, the company has completed 16 deals, contributing around $805 million in additional revenue. B. Riley said this disciplined M&A strategy will continue to expand SPX’s (SPXC) total addressable market and fuel future growth.

“We expect the company to continue to be a growth compounder,” Van Sinderen added, praising management’s execution and vision. “SPXC is a well-established, first-rate provider of HVAC and D&M solutions that is well-positioned for multi-year revenue growth and expanding profitability.”B. Riley set a price target of $195 a share on SPX (SPXC), based on an estimated enterprise value that's 16 times adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization for 2027.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: UNH, CSCO, NTES, FL, BABA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged lower on Thursday as investors digested early developments in the US-China trade tensions, alongside soft economic data and fresh monetary policy signals from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s address.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Cisco Systems (NASDAQ:CSCO) +4% — Shares rose after the company topped FQ3 estimates and issued strong guidance. Product orders jumped 20% Y/Y, with AI infrastructure orders exceeding $600M. For FQ4, Cisco guided revenue between $14.5B and $14.7B, with the midpoint above the $14.51B estimate, and projected adjusted EPS of $0.96–$0.98 vs. $0.95 consensus. It also raised its full-year revenue forecast to $56.5B–$56.7B (prior: $56B–$56.5B), topping the $56.4B consensus at the midpoint. FY non-GAAP EPS is now seen at $3.77–$3.79, in line with the $3.78 estimate.
  • Bionano Genomics (NASDAQ:BNGO) +20%— Shares jumped even as the company reported a 26% Y/Y revenue decline to $6.5M in Q1 2025. Despite the drop, Bionano improved gross margin to 46% from 32% a year earlier, driven by strategic cost cuts and product enhancements. Q1 operating expenses were slashed by 66% Y/Y. However, Bionano trimmed its 2025 revenue outlook to $26M–$30M from a prior range of $29M–$32M (vs. a consensus of $30.33M) and guided Q2 revenue to $6.3M–$6.8M, missing the $7.01M consensus. The company plans to install 15–20 new OGM systems this year.
  • Foot Locker (NYSE:FL) +81% — Shares skyrocketed after DICK'S Sporting Goods (NYSE:DKS) confirmed the acquisition of the footwear retailer in a ~$2.4B all-stock or cash deal, offering $24 per share, a 66% premium to FL’s 60-day VWAP. Foot Locker shareholders can choose either cash or 0.1168 shares of DKS stock for each FL share. The transaction, expected to close in H2 2025, is aimed at expanding DICK’S U.S. footprint and enabling its international entry. DICK’S expects $100M–$125M in medium-term synergies and EPS accretion post-close, excluding one-time costs. However, DICK’S Sporting Goods (NYSE:DKS) shares fell 9% following the announcement.
  • NetEase (NASDAQ:NTES) +14% — Shares jumped after the Chinese internet and gaming company delivered Q1 results that beat profit and revenue expectations, driven by double-digit growth in its core games segment, which offset softness in other areas. CEO William Ding credited the momentum to ongoing innovation, strong new title launches, and the continued success of long-standing franchises bolstered by fresh content updates and enhanced gameplay.
  • Deere (NYSE:DE) +6% — Shares rose after the equipment maker posted stronger-than-expected FQ2 results, even as sales fell 16% Y/Y and demand for agricultural machinery remained soft. The company trimmed the lower end of its FY2025 profit guidance to a range of $4.75B–$5.5B (from $5B–$5.5B) amid global trade tensions, weaker farm conditions, and market volatility. Deere cited softer crop prices, rising equipment inventories, and lingering tariff impacts as key headwinds.

     

  •  

Biggest stock losers

  • Luminar Technologies (NASDAQ:LAZR) -10% — Shares sank after the company reported mixed Q1 results and announced a leadership shake-up. Paul Ricci, former Nuance CEO, will take over as chief executive around May 21, 2025, following the resignation of founder Austin Russell as CEO and board chair due to a code of conduct inquiry by the board’s audit committee. The company emphasized that the issue does not impact financial results, and Russell will remain on the board. Luminar also said it expects to reduce quarterly non-GAAP OpEx by ~50% by the end of FY2025 compared to the start of FY2024.
  • New Fortress Energy (NASDAQ:NFE) -31%— Shares plummeted after the company missed Q1 expectations, posting a GAAP EPS loss of -$0.73 (missed by $0.57) and revenue of $470.5M (down 31.8% Y/Y, missing by $144M). The sharp decline was driven by weak performance across segments. Looking ahead, NFE expects core earnings to improve with new developments in Brazil and Nicaragua and Puerto Rico expansions. The 624 MW CELBA plant in Brazil is ~95% complete and expected to contribute to earnings in Q3.
  • Alibaba Group (NYSE:BABA) -6% — Shares fell after the Chinese e-commerce giant reported disappointing FQ4 results, missing on both top and bottom lines. While revenue rose 7% and EBITA jumped 36%, free cash flow dropped 76% Y/Y, driven primarily by increased spending on cloud infrastructure.
  • UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) -8% — Shares tumbled after reports surfaced that the Department of Justice is investigating the company’s Medicare Advantage operations for potential fraud, including allegations of improper billing practices. The DOJ’s healthcare-fraud unit is leading the probe, which adds to mounting challenges for the insurer following a surprise CEO shake-up earlier this week and an investor lawsuit alleging the company concealed the fallout from the murder of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson.

data.title: Jana Partners adds Freshpet, exits Wolfspeed, among other Q1 moves
data.description:
Jana Partners added Freshpet (NASDAQ:FRPT) in Q1, purchasing 735K shares, according to its latest 13F filing. 

The activist hedge fund exited its stake in Wolfspeed (NYSE:WOLF), selling 4.99 million shares,  according to the 13F filing on Thursday.

Jana increased its position in Markel Group (MKL) in Q1, Rapid7 (RPD) and Lamb Weston (LW). The hedge fund decreased its stake in Trimble (TRMB), according to the 13F filing. 

Shares of Freshpet (FRPR) rose 2.3% on Wednesday.

data.title: Lifeward reaffirms $28M–$30M 2025 revenue guidance as pipeline expands 70% and cost controls tighten
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lifeward Ltd. (LFWD) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Larry Jasinski opened the call by emphasizing Lifeward's commitment to profitable revenue growth, "tight expense control and cash management and a smooth leadership transition." Jasinski highlighted Q1 revenues of $5 million, noting the company's seasonal business and that Q1 is typically the slowest quarter. He stated, "We have made important progress that will propel growth in the subsequent quarters in the United States, including the FDA clearance of the ReWalk 7, our next generation exoskeleton. The first approval of a claim for ReWalk 7 by a commercial health insurance company, a partnership with CorLife to drive profitable progress in the workers' compensation segment and expansion of our MyoCycle distribution rates which are highly synergistic for our field sales team."
  • Jasinski detailed international progress, mentioning the ReWalk contract with BARMER in Germany and the expansion of partnerships with AlterG distributors. He stated, "Key measurements are- we have over 120 qualified ReWalk leads in our US pipeline, up over 70% up from just two quarters ago. We also have a record number of 36 ReWalk rentals underway, primarily in Germany, and these rentals are the leading indicator of future sales. AlterG is growing with the new NEO line and has grown by 19% and 17% in the last two quarters."
  • CFO Mike Lawless reported, "Lifeward reported revenue of $5 million in the first quarter of 2025 compared to $5.3 million in the first quarter 2024." Lawless noted, "In the most recent quarter, revenue from sales of former ReWalk Robotics products and services including ReWalk Exoskeletons, MyoCycles and ReStore Exo-Suits was $1.6 million while revenue from AlterG products and services was $3.4 million."
  • Lawless highlighted a "GAAP gross margin was 42.2% compared to 26.4% in the first quarter of 2024" and stated operating expenses were reduced, "GAAP operating expenses were $7 million for the first quarter of 2025 compared to $7.9 million in the first quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • Jasinski reaffirmed, "I reaffirm our guidance of sales between $28 million to $30 million for 2025 and our anticipation that the combined effect for the full year of growing revenue and declining operating expenses will result in a Q4 adjusted operating loss of approximately $1 million."
  • Jasinski expressed confidence in the pipeline, citing "momentum we see first in the AlterG product line, growing, as I said, 17% to 19% in the last two quarters. And on the ReWalk product line, between the depth of leads that we have in the United States, the rentals that we see in Germany and the addition of a significant partnership with CorLife, plus our first commercial pay are all factors that see this growing."

Financial Results

  • Lawless provided a breakdown: "revenue from sales of former ReWalk Robotics products and services including ReWalk Exoskeletons, MyoCycles and ReStore Exo-Suits was $1.6 million while revenue from AlterG products and services was $3.4 million." He noted a "strong first quarter for the AlterG product line with growth of 19% versus the first quarter of 2024, driven by continued robust performance from sales to international customers."
  • Lawless stated, "Our number of ReWalk cases in process in the United States rose to more than 125 qualified candidates for future claims submissions, up over 70% in the past two quarters, while Germany had 47 cases in process at the end of Q1, up 3 from the end of 2024."
  • The company ended the quarter with $5.7 million in cash and equivalents and no debt, with an additional $500,000 raised through the ATM facility after quarter end.

Q&A

  • Yale Jen, Laidlaw and Company, asked about the impact of recent tariffs on international business. Jasinski responded, "We certainly are well spread out for both terms of supply and revenue across the world. So this is something that we've monitored very carefully... As the ReWalk is primarily produced in Israel, it is not being affected at the same level, and we've been reasonably stable there."
  • Jen questioned the confidence behind maintaining full-year guidance despite a seasonally low Q1. Jasinski answered, "Our confidence is very much based on the momentum we see first in the AlterG product line, growing, as I said, 17% to 19% in the last two quarters. And on the ReWalk product line, between the depth of leads that we have in the United States, the rentals that we see in Germany and the addition of a significant partnership with CorLife, plus our first commercial pay are all factors that see this growing."
  • Swayampakula Ramakanth, H. C. Wainwright, asked about differences between ReWalk 7 and 6, price point, and reimbursement. Jasinski replied, "There are changes throughout the system... We've made it more powerful with a greater level of amperage and power within the system with larger batteries. And also we've gone to an off the shelf battery, which is safer predictable and easier to manage... it's essentially still the same price and it probably should be higher, would be my mindset. But CMS set the price and it works well."
  • Ben Haynor, Lake Street Capital Markets, inquired about the predictability of DME MACs criteria for claims. Jasinski stated, "Importantly, what we've seen in the past few months is time speaking with the MACs. They really have started to standardize and give us a reason and what they wanted to see in definitions that made this flow through as a brace and an orthotic device."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a slightly positive yet cautious tone, focusing on tariff impact, guidance credibility, and the practical implications of pipeline growth and product launches.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing operational improvements and pipeline strength, while in Q&A responses, Jasinski provided detailed answers and demonstrated assurance in overcoming challenges, particularly regarding regulatory and reimbursement processes. This confidence is reflected in phrases such as "we are on the right path and we will achieve steady progress towards our goals."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management continued with a constructive and forward-looking approach, with management's confidence level remaining high regarding revenue growth and cost controls.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for $28 million to $30 million in sales and a Q4 adjusted operating loss of approximately $1 million remains unchanged from the previous quarter, with continued emphasis on cost controls and profitable growth.
  • The US ReWalk pipeline grew to over 120 leads, up more than 70% in two quarters, compared to over 110 leads reported last quarter. Active rentals increased from 27 to 36, and German cases rose from 44 to 47.
  • AlterG sales growth continued at 17–19% over recent quarters, with ongoing manufacturing and product improvements discussed more explicitly in this quarter.
  • Management tone remained confident, focusing on execution and operational discipline, while analysts continued to probe around pipeline conversion, revenue guidance, and cost reductions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing monitoring of international tariffs, with supply chain and revenue impact considered manageable.
  • Temporary supply constraints were noted for AlterG due to transition to contract manufacturing.
  • The company is working with Medicare Administrative Contractors to establish predictable claims approval processes, aiming to resolve past claims and shorten approval timelines.
  • Analysts questioned the predictability of regulatory and reimbursement cycles and the impact of seasonality on revenue.

Final Takeaway

Lifeward management maintains confidence in achieving $28 million to $30 million in 2025 sales and narrowing Q4 adjusted operating loss to approximately $1 million, supported by a robust and expanding pipeline, strong product launches, and disciplined cost controls. The company highlights strong momentum in both the ReWalk and AlterG lines, ongoing international and commercial partnerships, and improved predictability in reimbursement cycles as key drivers for future profitable growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Thyssenkrupp plunges as quarterly adjusted EBIT sinks 90%
data.description:
Thyssenkrupp (OTCPK:TYEKF) (OTCPK:TKAMY) -12.7% in German trading Thursday after reporting FQ2 results that widely missed expectations, hurt by what it said was high economic uncertainty among most of its customers and regions due to higher tariffs.

Q2 adjusted EBIT plunged 90% Y/Y to €19M (~$21.2M), far lower than the €146M analyst estimate in a company-compiled consensus, as revenues fell 5% to €8.58B and orders shrank 6% to €8.08B, declining at all business units, excluding Marine Systems.

Thyssenkrupp (OTCPK:TYEKF) (OTCPK:TKAMY) is exposed to global trade frictions through its sprawling global structure, and warned on Thursday that tariffs would hurt global automotive production in 2025.

"The introduction of universal import tariffs and individual customs tariffs for major trading partners like the EU and China are having a negative impact on global trade and destabilising international supply chains," the company said.

Thyssenkrupp (OTCPK:TYEKF) (OTCPK:TKAMY) said its steel division swung to a loss of €23M, compared with a €68M profit in the year-earlier quarer, also hit by maintenance-related outages.

The company said it still expects adjusted EBIT of €600M-€1B and free cash flow before M&A between flat and €300M.
